metadata.title: Costco: Relentless Focus on the One Thing

conclusion: Fuss and Bloomstrand frame Costco as the original consumer subscription: ~55 million members pay $60 a year while the company marks goods up just 12–13% and returns scale savings to shoppers. Decades of process power — 4,000 SKUs, Kirkland at ~25% of sales, and ~$1,500 per square foot — make the membership flywheel hard to replicate.
background:
Matt Reustle breaks down Costco with Zach Fuss of Continental Grain and Chris Bloomstrand, CIO at Semper Augustus and a long-time shareholder. Fuss walks the core equation: ~800 warehouses across 12 countries doing roughly $180 billion in sales as the second-largest U.S. retailer behind Walmart's ~$550 billion, powered by a maniacal focus on lowest price for members.

The conversation covers the inverted economics where ~75% of operating income comes from membership fees rather than merchandise margin, Jim Sinegal's process power from pallet-ready packaging to 15-times inventory turns, and Kirkland private label at roughly 25% of sales. Bloomstrand adds international lessons from Iceland — where one store signed up the entire 350,000-person country — and Sinegal's long-term public-company mindset versus quarterly earnings pressure.
important_facts: Costco runs roughly 800 warehouses across 12 countries on about $180 billion of annual sales as the second-largest U.S. retailer behind Walmart at ~$550 billion. About 55 million members pay $60 a year with retention in the 90% range — 50 million paid members on ~100 million total store memberships — and Fuss estimates ~75% of operating income comes from membership fees, roughly $3.5 billion annually at ~80–90% incremental margin, not product markup.

Costco marks merchandise up 12–13%, backing into an ~11% gross margin versus 25–35% at typical retailers; it buys at $10 and sells at ~$11 while peers might charge $13–15 on comparable specs. Kirkland private label is ~25% of sales — standalone among the largest CPG businesses in America — with only ~4,000 SKUs versus ~40,000 at a grocer or ~100,000 at a Walmart supercenter, and Fuss notes Costco turns inventory ~15 times a year with negative working capital on much of the store base.

Sales per square foot have climbed from roughly $800–1,000 historically to ~$1,500 today — about 2.5–3× Walmart productivity — as basket size and traffic compound positive comps year after year. New warehouses cost $75–100 million including land and inventory, ramp from ~$100–150 million to $200 million-plus in sales over 8–10 years, and generate roughly $8–10 million of free cash flow per store annually at ~3% store-level margins.

mental_model.name: Membership-Funded Scale Economies Shared
mental_model.components: Charge a recurring membership fee, keep merchandise margins near break-even, and pass vendor negotiating leverage back to members as lower prices. Fuss calls Costco the original consumer subscription — before CAC/LTV parlance — allegedly inspiring Amazon Prime. The flywheel: low prices attract members, higher member counts increase purchasing scale, better vendor terms fund further price cuts. Process power reinforces the loop: pallet-ready packaging eliminates backrooms, bulk sizing raises basket size and cuts shrink, and industrial-site locations lower rent — savings reinvested into wages above $20/hour and the $1.50 hot dog.
mental_model.application: Retailers competing on merchandise margin alone cannot match a model where membership subsidizes ~1% product margin while comps stay positive year after year. Bloomstrand's Iceland example shows the playbook travels: beat local grocers on unit price even when absolute dollars are higher than U.S. warehouses. Operators outside retail can borrow the 'one thing' discipline — Sinegal refused hot-dog price increases and vertically integrated rotisserie chickens when Tyson birds grew too large — but replicating 40 years of culture and supplier trust is the hard part.

key_insights.view: Membership income, not markup, drives profitability.
key_insights.question: How does Costco make money?
key_insights.answer: Fuss says ~75% of operating income comes from ~55 million members paying $60 a year at ~90% retention, flowing through at ~80–90% incremental margin. Merchandise is marked up only 12–13% for an ~11% gross margin — Costco acquires at $10 and sells near $11 while Walmart might charge $13–15 on the same item.

key_insights.view: Kirkland turns brand equity into margin without raising price.
key_insights.question: Why is private label central?
key_insights.answer: Kirkland is ~25% of sales and disintermediates national brands so Costco can charge less yet earn more per unit — quality often matches or beats branded peers (vodka, golf balls). With only ~4,000 SKUs versus ~40,000 at a grocer, every item is intentional and the Kirkland name carries the trust to convert trials.

key_insights.view: Long store maturation requires patient capital.
key_insights.question: What makes unit economics distinctive?
key_insights.answer: Bloomstrand notes new stores cost $75–100 million, start around $130 million in revenue, and mature above $200 million over 8–10 years while returning only ~$8–10 million of annual free cash flow per site. Sinegal ignored short-term ROE critiques because half the chain was still ramping — a private-company mindset inside a public stock.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Recurring membership fees fund sub-11% merchandise margins while ~$1,500 per-square-foot productivity and positive same-store comps compound; Bloomstrand argues mature-store economics were masked for years by a ramping base of younger warehouses.

Walmart's everyday-low-price model still runs 25–35% gross margins and ~30% back-of-house footprint versus Costco's no-backroom pallet model — Fuss argues the membership-funded ~11% gross margin structure is an impenetrable retail moat.

golden_quotes: Fuss calls Costco the original consumer subscription — 55 million members paying $60 a year before CAC-to-LTV became investor parlance, allegedly inspiring Amazon Prime.
Sinegal reportedly told executives not to change the hot-dog price — a symbol of passing every saved penny back to members rather than extracting margin.
Bloomstrand on Iceland: one warehouse signed up the entire 350,000-person country by beating local grocers on unit price even when dollar prices exceeded U.S. levels.

events.event: Founded in Seattle by Jim Sinegal and Jeff Brotman on membership warehouse retail.
Scales to hundreds of U.S. warehouses; Kirkland private label grows to ~25% of sales with ~4,000 SKUs.
Bloomstrand buys in when ~350 stores include many still-maturing units masking headline return on capital.
International expansion accelerates across Canada, Asia, Europe; opens first China store in Shanghai to overnight lines.
Opens Reykjavik, Iceland — one store captures the 350,000-person market by undercutting local grocers.
Reustle hosts EP.4 breakdown with Fuss and Bloomstrand as Costco nears ~800 warehouses and ~$180 billion in sales.
